After much negociation, Secretary M’Grath
managed to book the Limerick Market
Fields for Sunday 30th inst. and proceeded
to advertise the fixture for that date and
venue, Now, however, there comes the
news that the Limerick ground has been
cor verted into a Cavalry encampment, and
as everyone will admit, a horse-trodden
turf is not quite suitable for the niceties of
hurling. The fixture has again been can-
celled, but an endeavour will be made to
find some other suitable venue,

The Connacht hurling final takes place
between Roscommon and Galway on Sun-
day next at Roscommon, For some yeus
past these have been the only counties to
put forward teams for the Connacht chams
pionship, but their annual meeting has been
nope the less interesting by reason of this.
Last year Roscommon defeated Galway by
3.4 to 3-2, thereby dispossessing the latter
of the title for the fist time since 1887.
Problem—will it be another 26 years be-
fore Roscommon defeats Galway again ?

The replayed Ulster semi-final between
Monaghan and Armagh took plac: on
Sunday last at Culloville, and as was the
casein the former match (which resulted
ina scoreless draw) the scoring. was low.
Both sides registered twice, but Monaghan
had a goal and» point to their credit while

ATHLETICS AND CYCLING

World’s Record for 56Ibs.
Push. -

‘The magnificent weighthrowing power*
of O'Grady, Caherconlish, were exhibited
to perfection at Kilmallock sports, whee
he set up a new world’s record mark for the
sélbs push. of a century
Willie Real's (Pallasgreen) record of 23ft.
gin. for this event was unapproached, but
fast year at Kilmallock J. O'Grady’ and

M. O'Brien, Bulgaden, sent the missile

two inches farther, and tied for the world’s
record at 23f, t1in. This year again
Kilmallock was favoured by a further
notable performance, as O'Grady exceeded
his previous best effort by four inches, and
set up anew world’s record at 24ft. 3in

M. O'Brica, Bulgaden, who tied with
O'Grady last year, was alto. a competitor
fon this occasion, but he was well below
Brevious form, and only figured in the third
place, almost three feet ochind the cham-
pion. The weight, performances of J.
Bresnahan, Castletowaconyers, in the put-
ting 28lbs open handicap (Which he won)
and in the s6lbs championship (which
O'Grady annexed) are worthy of notice,
and there would appear,to be. further
hhonours in store for this competition,

Some fast cycling took place at the meet»
ing, as might only be expected from such
Ayers as O'Sullivan (Doneraile),.Byme
(Dublin), Barnes (Thomastown), Jones
(Castleisland), Quinlan (Kilfioane), ete,
‘O'Sullivan proved to be the “crack” of the
meeting, a8 he won the mile handicap and
three miles scratch in capital style, Jones
secured the five miles championship.

J. Horan, Birr, G.A.A. four miles cham-
pion, proved useful over two miles also, as
he won the G.A.A. championship at tbat
distance from J. J. Ryan, Tlppetary.
Pigott, Gort, ran in third,

MALLOW.

Mallow sports were held on Saturday
last the attendance being on the small side,
owing to the curtailment of travelling
facilities. A pleasurable feature of the pro-
gramme was the re-appearance of T. J.
Leahy, Charleville, who competed in, and
won, the jumping events listed. In the
high jump“ Tim” cleared the lath at 6 fe.
even, and in the pole jump he reached 11ft.

tin, Both performances show that he is in
first-rate form, and it is to be hoped we
will see him’in action again before the

scason ends. In the weight events J. Bres-
nahan, Charleville, outdistanced his com-
petitors, putting up moderately good per-
formances,

There were two Munster championship
events on the programme, the 220. yards
flat and one mile cycle, M. Walsh,
Kilfinane, won a good race on the tape in
the former event. J. O'Sullivan, Done-
taile, who is holder of the All-Ireland ticle
for the mile this year, had an easy victory
in the wheel event,
